sections.text: Welcome to AP Biology, Module Thirteen — Community Ecology. This sits inside College Board Unit Eight, Ecology, which is ten to fifteen percent of your exam. Module Twelve looked at single populations. Today we zoom out to the whole community — how different species interact, why some coexist and others get pushed out, and what happens when a community is disturbed.

Ecologist Robert Paine pulled one species out of a tidal zone — a sea star, Pisaster ochraceus, not even the most abundant animal there. Yet the diverse community collapsed into a wall of mussels and almost nothing else. How can removing one uncommon species break a whole community? That's the puzzle of community ecology, and we'll answer it today.

Three goals today. One — how species interact, the six interaction types and their plus-minus-zero signs, and what competition does to who lives where. Two — how predators, prey, and their defenses shape each other, including the outsized role of keystone species. Three — how communities change over time through succession, and how we actually measure their diversity and stability.

Here's the master table — six interactions, scored by effect on each species. Competition hurts both, minus-minus, like two warbler species over one tree. Predation, herbivory, and parasitism are all plus-minus — lynx and hare, deer and plant, tapeworm and host. Mutualism is plus-plus, coral and zooxanthellae or mycorrhizae and plant roots. Commensalism is plus-zero — a barnacle rides a whale, unaffected. Keep each sign attached to the right organism even if you swap columns.

Here's the AP trap. First confirm one species clearly benefits — a plus. Once it does, mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism differ only in the second species. Mutualism: both win, plus-plus. Commensalism: one wins, one unaffected, plus-zero — the barnacle on the whale. Parasitism: one wins, one harmed, plus-minus. But watch out: if neither benefits, it's competition, minus-minus — the second-species trick doesn't apply there.

Gause's competitive exclusion principle says two species fighting over the exact same limiting resource can't coexist forever — one always outcompetes the other. So how do similar species ever live together? Resource partitioning. Robert MacArthur found warbler species sharing one spruce tree, but each feeding in a different part of it. By splitting the resource, they both survive — same tree, different niches.

Two niche terms the AP loves to confuse. The fundamental niche is everything a species could do — the full range it can tolerate with no competitors around. The realized niche is what it actually does once negative interactions — chiefly competition, plus predation — push it out of part of that range. The realized niche always fits inside the fundamental niche; these interactions shrink it, never expand it. If an answer says competition expanded a niche, it's wrong.

Predators and prey can lock into cycles. The Lotka-Volterra equations predict that predator and prey populations oscillate up and down over time, slightly out of phase — prey rise, predators follow, prey crash, predators crash. The classic real-world data: Canada lynx and snowshoe hare, reconstructed from Hudson's Bay fur-trading records, which show roughly ten-year cycles. The model captures the oscillation pattern, though real cycles involve more than these two species alone.

Prey defend themselves — camouflage, called cryptic coloration; aposematic warning coloration, like poison dart frogs; plus behavioral and chemical defenses. Then mimicry. Batesian is a bluff — a palatable, undefended species copies an unpalatable, defended one; only the model is defended, so the mimic freeloads, effectively parasitic. The classic viceroy-and-monarch pair was later reconsidered, but the term still applies to many cases. Müllerian differs — multiple defended species share one warning pattern, so the predator learns once and all pay less, effectively mutualistic.

Your turn. Scenario A — a totally undefended fly evolves to look like a stinging wasp; the fly is harmless, the wasp is defended. Scenario B — several genuinely toxic, defended butterfly species all converge on the same bold orange-and-black pattern. Which one is Batesian mimicry, which is Müllerian, and what's the reason for each? Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Here's the breakdown. Scenario A is Batesian — an undefended fly mimicking a defended wasp. Only one is defended, so the mimic bluffs, freeloading on the predator's avoidance — effectively parasitic. Scenario B is Müllerian — multiple defended species sharing one warning pattern; the predator learns once and all benefit — effectively mutualistic. Undefended-copies-defended is Batesian; many-defended-share-a-signal is Müllerian.

Now the opening puzzle. A keystone species has an effect disproportionately large relative to its abundance — not defined by being the most common or largest. That's why removing Paine's rare sea star, Pisaster ochraceus, let mussels take over and crashed biodiversity. Yellowstone's wolves act top-down, producing a trophic cascade — but that cascade is the mechanism, the effect, not a synonym for keystone. A keystone could just as easily be a mutualist or ecosystem engineer.

Succession is the predictable change in a community after a disturbance, and the AP wants a clean split. Primary succession starts on bare rock with no soil — a fresh volcanic island, or land exposed by glacial retreat; lichens and mosses pioneer, building soil over centuries. Secondary succession happens where soil remains after a fire or abandoned farm, so it's much faster — herbaceous plants, then shrubs, then forest. The dividing line is soil, not the disturbance.

Diversity is not just a head-count. Species richness is how many species there are. Species evenness is how equal their abundances are — a community dominated by one species is uneven despite a long list. The Shannon index, H, rolls both together. And it matters: higher diversity is generally associated with a more stable ecosystem — more resistant and resilient, often more productive, and harder to invade. Treat that as a correlation, not a guarantee.

Recap. Competition minus-minus; predation, herbivory, parasitism all plus-minus; mutualism plus-plus; commensalism plus-zero — once one species benefits, read the second's sign; if neither does, it's competition. Competitive exclusion versus resource partitioning, with MacArthur's warblers. The realized niche sits inside the fundamental, shrunk by negative interactions. Lotka-Volterra predicts cycles, like lynx and hare. Batesian is palatable-mimics-defended; Müllerian, many-defended-sharing-a-pattern. Keystone means disproportionate impact, not abundance — the trophic cascade is the mechanism. Primary succession: bare rock; secondary keeps soil. Diversity is richness plus evenness via Shannon's H.

Your path forward. Read the OpenStax Biology ecology chapters for community ecology, biodiversity, and disruptions to ecosystems. Practice with the Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Eight problem sets — interaction signs, niches, succession, and diversity questions are highly predictable. Then open your GIIS dashboard, complete the Unit Eight assignment, and book an advisor check-in if Batesian versus Müllerian or primary versus secondary succession still feels fuzzy. Next up: Module Fourteen — Ecosystems and Energy Flow. See you there.
